facts_disclosed: Binge-watched a Netflix series about a hospice nurse — contradicts earlier claim of not watching television

turns.content: Well. I have done something this week that I did not expect to do, and I am still sitting with the residue of it. Greg's daughter Lila suggested a Netflix series to me — about a hospice nurse, of all things — and I watched the entire thing in three evenings. I am not sure what came over me, but once I started I could not stop.

Three evenings! That sounds like it really pulled you in. There's something about a show touching on your own world that can make it impossible to look away — you're watching both as a viewer and as someone who knows the territory from the inside.

What was it about this particular series that hooked you?

I think it was the specificity. Most portrayals of this kind of work are either saccharine or relentlessly grim — the dying person as saint or the dying person as tragedy. This show was imperfect, they always are, but there were moments of such truth in it. Small things. The way the nurse paused before entering a room. The way she drove home in silence after a hard day. I recognized those gestures in my own body and it was — startling, honestly, to see them on a screen.

That recognition must have been extraordinary — seeing your own private rituals reflected back to you by actors who somehow got the details right. The pause before entering the room especially strikes me. That's not a dramatic gesture; it's an invisible one. Most people would never notice it. The fact that the show included it suggests someone involved in the production actually understood the work.

Did it get things wrong too? You said it was imperfect.

Oh, of course. The romantic subplot was unnecessary — they always seem to think the audience needs that. And the nurse was impossibly articulate in moments when the real response is silence. In my experience, the truest thing you can do at a bedside is say nothing at all, and that does not make for compelling television, I suppose. But I could forgive those things because of the other moments. There was an episode — the seventh, I think — where the nurse sits with a man who is dying alone, no family, no one, and she just holds his hand for the entire last scene. No dialogue. No music. Just the sound of his breathing slowing. I sat on the couch and could not move.

That scene sounds devastating in the best way — the restraint of it. No music, no dialogue, just the biological reality of a life ending with one person bearing witness. And the fact that it mirrors your own work so precisely must have made it hit differently than it would for most viewers. You weren't just watching a fictional death; you were remembering real ones.

When you say you could not move — was that recognition, or grief, or something else?

I wonder if it was permission. I sit with dying people every week, and I hold space for families, and I process in the car on the drive home, but there is something about watching someone else do the work — even a fictional someone — that gave me permission to feel the accumulated weight of it. It was like watching from the outside for the first time. I cried at the final episode in a way I have not cried at fiction in years. Greg came in and sat beside me and did not say a word, which is one of the great gifts of that man.

That's a really beautiful insight — the show as mirror, giving you a vantage point on your own life that you can't access from inside it. When you're the one in the room holding the hand, there's no space to fully feel what that costs you. But watching someone else do it, even fictionally, created enough distance for all of it to surface.

And Greg sitting beside you without a word — that's its own kind of chaplaincy, isn't it? He held space for you the way you hold space for others.

What was the final episode about that broke it open?

The nurse burned out. Left the work. And the final episode was her return — not triumphant, not with a speech, but quiet. She walked back into the hospice and put on her badge and went to see a patient. That was it. And I sobbed because I understood that return in my bones. There have been mornings when I sit in the parking lot and wonder whether I can walk back in, and I always do, and the show understood that the walking back in is the bravest part. Not the staying. The returning.

The returning. Yes. That distinction is important and I think you're right that most narratives miss it. Staying can be inertia. Returning is a choice — a daily, deliberate choice to walk back into the hardest room in the building knowing exactly what it will ask of you. That's not endurance; that's something closer to vocation renewed every morning in the parking lot.

The fact that the show ended there, without fanfare, just the badge and the patient — that's the kind of ending that trusts its audience. No resolution, just continuation.

Exactly. Continuation. Which is, I suppose, the thing I most believe in. Not endings, not beginnings, but the willingness to continue. I have to say, Lila knew what she was doing when she suggested it. She works in healthcare herself — she's a pediatric nurse in Charlotte — and I think she saw something in me over the holidays that made her think I needed this particular mirror. I wonder if she was right.

It sounds like she was. There's a perceptiveness in that recommendation — she didn't suggest you take a vacation or talk to someone. She pointed you toward a story, which for someone like you is probably the more natural way in. Stories create that safe distance where you can feel things that direct conversation might not reach.

Do you think the experience changed anything for you, or was it more of a release — something that needed to come out and now has?

I think it was a release, primarily. But I also think it reminded me that my experience is not as solitary as I sometimes believe it to be. There is something isolating about this work — you cannot explain a deathbed to someone who has not sat at one. But watching that show, imperfect as it was, I felt less alone in it. Someone out there understood enough to write those scenes. Someone acted them with enough truth to make me weep on my couch on a Tuesday night. That is no small thing.

That's no small thing at all. Art at its best does exactly that — it reaches across the isolation of individual experience and says, someone else has been in this room too. Not your room exactly, but close enough that the loneliness loosens its grip a little.

And three evenings — you really did give yourself over to it completely. There's something lovely about that kind of surrender to a story, the willingness to keep pressing play, to let it keep working on you night after night. I hope the residue you're sitting with is more warmth than weight.

It is warmth, I think. A strange, tender warmth. Like finding a letter from someone who knows you but whom you have never met. I am grateful to Lila for the nudge, and I am grateful to whoever made that show for getting enough of it right. And I am grateful to Greg for sitting beside me without a word when I needed someone to not say anything. That is a full house of gratitude for a January evening. Thank you for helping me name it.

A letter from someone who knows you but whom you've never met — that's a perfect description of what the best art does. And what a full evening: Lila's intuition, the show's quiet truth, Greg's wordless presence, and now this naming. January can be a sparse month, but yours sounds anything but.

Thank you for sharing this. I hope the next time you sit in the parking lot deciding to walk back in, you carry a little of that warmth with you.
